region_of_origin: United States (American-style burger; served in Singapore by Taiwanese operator)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002383 (hamburger) is the closest parent. The smash burger variant with caramelised onions and milk bun is not individually coded in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Hamburger) is the closest defensible parent code. The specific smash-patty preparation and milk bun are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 (Hamburger) is the closest parent concept. Smash burger specifics are not individually coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One smash burger consisting of two smashed beef patties, American cheese, caramelised onions, and a milk bun, weighing approximately 220 g.

nutrient_source.title: Hamburger, double patty with condiments and vegetables (USDA SR Legacy NDB 21108) adapted for smash burger profile; American cheese slice from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 1097512; milk bun estimated from USDA enriched hamburger bun FDC ID 1082653
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for smash burger exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods for this specific product. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy double-patty hamburger (NDB 21108) as the primary macro anchor, with American cheese (FDC 1097512) and enriched hamburger bun (FDC 1082653) used to refine the composite. The smash technique increases surface-area browning (Maillard) but does not materially alter macronutrient composition relative to a standard double-patty burger. Caramelised onions contribute minor carbohydrate and sugar. Per-100g values are computed from a 220 g assembled serving estimate.

gi_source.title: Hamburger bun, white bread GI reference; mixed meal GI attenuation from fat and protein — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a smash burger. The GI is estimated at 54 based on: (1) white hamburger bun GI ~70 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) significant fat (14.8 g/100g) and protein (14.5 g/100g) content substantially attenuates the glycemic response of the mixed meal, consistent with Brand-Miller et al. mixed-meal GI attenuation principles; (3) caramelised onions contribute fructose which has low GI (~19). The composite mixed-meal GI estimate of 54 is consistent with published data for double-patty hamburgers in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (hamburger GI ~66 for bun-dominant assessment, reduced to ~54 when full fat/protein load is accounted for). GL per serving = (54 × 45.1 g available carbohydrate per 220 g serving) / 100 = 24.4, rounded to 24.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100g; ~1056 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit smash burger consumption due to the high sodium load (~1056 mg per serving from processed cheese, seasoned patty, and bun), which represents approximately 46% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; occasional consumption with low-sodium accompaniments is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~24 per serving) and refined carbohydrate from milk bun
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the milk bun contributes refined carbohydrates with a moderate glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving; pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side and monitoring portion size is recommended to manage postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.2 g/100g), trans fat (~0.4 g/100g), cholesterol (~52 mg/100g), and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit smash burger intake due to the combination of saturated fat (~13.6 g per serving), trans fat (~0.9 g per serving), and high sodium (~1056 mg per serving), all of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and elevated blood pressure; consumption should be infrequent and portion-controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A smash burger is an American-style burger prepared by pressing or 'smashing' a ball of ground beef forcefully onto a hot flat-top griddle, creating a thin patty with a highly caramelised, crispy crust through the Maillard reaction. The 'Smash It' variant served at Riverside Buns in Singapore features two smashed beef patties, a slice of American processed cheese melted directly onto the hot patty, sweet caramelised onions, and a soft, enriched milk bun. The milk bun adds a slightly sweet, pillowy texture that contrasts with the savoury, crispy-edged patties. The overall flavour profile is rich, umami-forward, and indulgent, with sweetness from the caramelised onions and bun. Nutritionally, the burger is energy-dense (~268 kcal/100g), with significant protein (~14.5 g/100g) from the beef and moderate-to-high fat (~14.8 g/100g) including saturated fat (~6.2 g/100g) from both the beef and American cheese. Sodium content is high (~480 mg/100g), making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycemic load per serving is moderate (~24), driven primarily by the refined carbohydrate in the milk bun. Clinically, this food is high concern for cardiovascular disease and hypertension due to saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its glycemic load. It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal-unverified, or gluten-free diets. The dish is sold at SGD 12 at Riverside Buns, Boat Quay, Singapore, and is tagged under Taiwanese, Burger, and Sandwich cuisine categories on field survey data.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a smash burger?
Is smash burger high in sodium?
Smash It burger field survey data

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for this specific smash burger product in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Branded Foods, or ICMR-NIN). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy double-patty hamburger data and component-level USDA FoodData Central entries for American cheese and enriched hamburger bun. The GI is an analogous estimate based on published mixed-meal GI attenuation principles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy hamburger source entry used for this adaptation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source for this composite. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source for this composite burger. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. The food_name has been normalised from the menu name 'Smash It' to the canonical English food name 'Smash Burger' for database purposes; 'Smash It' is retained as a primary alias. Cuisine is listed as 'American-style / Taiwanese-operated' reflecting that the burger style is American while the operator (Riverside Buns) is tagged as Taiwanese on field survey. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Singapore-served dish. The GI estimate of 54 places this food in the low GI category (≤55) per the validator threshold. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 45.1 g available carbohydrate in 220 g serving) / 100 ≈ 24.1.
